questions.q: A morphism of schemes $f\colon X\to Y$ is a continuous map $|X|\to|Y|$ together with a map of sheaves of rings $f^{\#}\colon \mathcal{O}_Y\to f_{*}\mathcal{O}_X$ satisfying which extra condition?
choices: $f^{\#}$ is an isomorphism of sheaves
the induced map on stalks $f^{\#}_x\colon \mathcal{O}_{Y,f(x)}\to \mathcal{O}_{X,x}$ is a local homomorphism (sends maximal ideal into maximal ideal)
$f$ is a homeomorphism onto its image
$f^{\#}$ is surjective on every open set
questions.explain: A morphism of schemes is a morphism of locally ringed spaces: the stalk maps $f^{\#}_x$ must be local, i.e. send the maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}_{f(x)}$ into $\mathfrak{m}_x$. Without this, arbitrary ringed-space maps between spectra don't correspond to ring maps.

questions.q: For affine schemes, what is the anti-equivalence between rings and affine schemes?
choices: Ring homomorphisms $A\to B$ correspond to scheme morphisms $\operatorname{Spec} A\to \operatorname{Spec} B$ (same direction)
Ring homomorphisms $A\to B$ correspond to scheme morphisms $\operatorname{Spec} B\to \operatorname{Spec} A$ (reversed direction)
Ring homomorphisms correspond to continuous maps only, not scheme morphisms
Every continuous map of spectra comes from a unique ring map
questions.hint: $\operatorname{Spec}$ is a contravariant functor: a ring map $\varphi\colon A\to B$ induces $\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\colon \operatorname{Spec} B\to\operatorname{Spec} A$ by pulling back prime…
questions.explain: $\operatorname{Spec}$ is a contravariant functor: a ring map $\varphi\colon A\to B$ induces $\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\colon \operatorname{Spec} B\to\operatorname{Spec} A$ by pulling back prime ideals via $\mathfrak{q}\mapsto \varphi^{-1}(\mathfrak{q})$.

questions.q: How many morphisms $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_5\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)$ over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ are there? (Equivalently, how many ring maps $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-1)\to \mathbb{F}_5$ fixing $\mathbb{Z}$?)
questions.explain: Such a ring map is determined by the image of $x$, which must satisfy $x^2=1$ in $\mathbb{F}_5$. The solutions are $x=\pm 1$, so there are $2$ morphisms.

hard.q: Consider the ringed-space map $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ defined on the underlying space as the identity but with $f^{\#}\colon \mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ being complex conjugation. Why is this NOT a morphism of schemes over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$?
choices: Complex conjugation is not a ring homomorphism
Complex conjugation is a ring automorphism of $\mathbb{C}$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, so this IS a valid scheme morphism over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$
The stalk map is not local because it sends the maximal ideal to zero
The underlying continuous map is not the identity
hard.hint: Morphisms of affine schemes correspond to opposite ring maps.
hard.explain: Complex conjugation $\sigma\colon\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ is a $\mathbb{Q}$-algebra automorphism (it fixes $\mathbb{Q}\subset\mathbb{R}$) and sends the unique maximal ideal $(0)$ to $(0)$, so the stalk map is local. It defines a perfectly valid non-trivial automorphism of $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ over $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$.

hard.q: How many ring maps $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\to\mathbb{F}_3$ are there? (Hint: how many solutions does $x^2\equiv -1\pmod 3$ have?)
hard.explain: We need $x^2\equiv -1\equiv 2\pmod 3$. Checking: $0^2=0$, $1^2=1$, $2^2=4\equiv 1$. None equals $2$, so $x^2+1$ has no roots in $\mathbb{F}_3$, and there are $0$ ring maps, meaning no morphisms $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_3\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[i]$.

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct chain of implications for a morphism of locally ringed spaces $f\colon(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\to(Y,\mathcal{O}_Y)$?
choices: Continuous map $+$ sheaf map $\Rightarrow$ locally ringed space morphism, with no further condition
Continuous map $+$ sheaf map of rings $+$ locality of stalk maps $\Rightarrow$ locally ringed space morphism $\Rightarrow$ (if both are schemes) scheme morphism
Homeomorphism $+$ isomorphism of sheaves $\Rightarrow$ scheme morphism
Any continuous map between the underlying topological spaces extends to a scheme morphism
hard.hint: Local ring maps send maximal ideals into maximal ideals.
hard.explain: A morphism of ringed spaces is a continuous map plus a map $f^{\#}\colon\mathcal{O}_Y\to f_*\mathcal{O}_X$. It is a morphism of locally ringed spaces iff additionally each stalk map $f^{\#}_x$ is local. When source and target are schemes this is exactly the correct notion of scheme morphism.

questions.q: A morphism $i\colon Z\to X$ is a closed immersion iff:
choices: $i$ is injective on points
$i$ is a homeomorphism onto a closed subset of $X$ and $i^{\#}\colon \mathcal{O}_X\to i_{*}\mathcal{O}_Z$ is surjective
$i$ is open and injective
$i$ is proper and finite
questions.explain: Closed immersion = topological closed inclusion + surjective map of structure sheaves. The kernel of $i^{\#}$ is the ideal sheaf cutting out $Z$.

questions.q: Which ring map corresponds to the open immersion $D(f)\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} A$?
choices: $A\twoheadrightarrow A/(f)$ (quotient)
$A\to A_f$ (localization at $f$)
$A\hookrightarrow A[x]$ (polynomial ring)
$A\to A\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} A$ (tensor square)
questions.explain: Open immersions onto a basic open come from localization: $A\to A_f = A[1/f]$ inverts $f$, and primes of $A_f$ correspond exactly to primes of $A$ not containing $f$.

questions.q: The closed subscheme $V((x^2))\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} k[x]$ has the same underlying topological space as $V((x))$ (just the origin), but a different structure sheaf. What is $\dim_k \Gamma(V((x^2)),\mathcal{O}) = \dim_k k[x]/(x^2)$?
questions.explain: $k[x]/(x^2)$ has $k$-basis $\{1,x\}$, so dimension $2$. This is the 'fat point' / tangent direction attached to the origin — a non-reduced closed subscheme.

hard.q: Let $Z\hookrightarrow X$ be a closed immersion with ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}$, and let $U\hookrightarrow X$ be the complementary open immersion onto $X\setminus Z$. Which sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules is exact?
choices: $0\to\mathcal{O}_Z\to\mathcal{O}_X\to j_*\mathcal{O}_U\to 0$
$0\to\mathcal{I}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to i_*\mathcal{O}_Z\to 0$
$0\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{I}\to i_*\mathcal{O}_Z\to 0$
$0\to i_*\mathcal{O}_Z\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{I}\to 0$
hard.hint: The open complement has structure sheaf equal to $\mathcal{O}_X$ restricted.
hard.explain: The ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}\subset\mathcal{O}_X$ is the kernel of $i^{\#}\colon\mathcal{O}_X\twoheadrightarrow i_*\mathcal{O}_Z$, giving the short exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{I}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to i_*\mathcal{O}_Z\to 0$. This is the fundamental way closed subschemes encode their 'thickness' via ideal sheaves.

hard.q: Which is a valid counterexample showing that open immersions need NOT be affine?
choices: $D(f)\hookrightarrow\operatorname{Spec} A$ — this is always affine
The complement of a codimension-$2$ closed subvariety in $\mathbb{A}^n_k$ for $n\ge 2$ is an open immersion that is not affine when $n=2$ and the removed set is a point
Every open immersion of affine schemes is again affine
The open subscheme $\mathbb{P}^1_k\setminus\{P\}\cong\mathbb{A}^1_k$ — this is not affine
hard.explain: $\mathbb{A}^2_k\setminus\{(0,0)\}$ is an open subscheme of $\mathbb{A}^2_k$ but is not affine: its global sections are still $k[x,y]$ (by Hartogs), yet it is not isomorphic to $\operatorname{Spec} k[x,y]$ because it is not affine (the structure sheaf has the right global sections but the space is not affine by Serre's criterion).

hard.q: The $n$-th infinitesimal neighborhood of the origin in $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ is $\operatorname{Spec} k[x]/(x^n)$. What is $\dim_k k[x]/(x^3)$?
hard.hint: Compute dimension as $n$ using the basis $1, x, \ldots, x^{n-1}$.
hard.explain: $k[x]/(x^3)$ has $k$-basis $\{1,x,x^2\}$, dimension $3$. Generally the $n$-th infinitesimal neighborhood has $\dim_k = n$.

questions.q: In the category of affine schemes, the fiber product $\operatorname{Spec} A \times_{\operatorname{Spec} R} \operatorname{Spec} B$ is represented by which ring?
choices: $A\times B$ (direct product)
$A\oplus_R B$ (direct sum as $R$-modules)
$A\otimes_R B$ (tensor product over $R$)
$A/R \cdot B/R$
questions.explain: Fiber product is the categorical pullback; on affines it's dual to the pushout in rings, which is the tensor product: $\operatorname{Spec} A\times_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\operatorname{Spec} B = \operatorname{Spec}(A\otimes_R B)$.

questions.q: Which property is preserved under arbitrary base change?
choices: being a bijection on underlying topological spaces
being a closed immersion
being surjective on closed points of a specific field
having exactly one point in every fiber
questions.explain: Closed immersions are preserved by base change: if $Z\hookrightarrow X$ is closed and $X'\to X$ any morphism, then $Z\times_X X'\hookrightarrow X'$ is again a closed immersion. Topological bijectivity and point-count in fibers are not stable under base change.

questions.q: As a $\mathbb{Q}$-vector space, what is the dimension of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})\otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$ has $\mathbb{Q}$-dimension $2$, so the tensor product has dimension $2\cdot 2 = 4$. Geometrically, $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$ is $4$-dimensional over $\mathbb{Q}$ and splits as two points over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)$.

hard.q: Compute $\mathbb{F}_p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{F}_p$ as a ring. Which option is correct?
choices: $\mathbb{F}_p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{F}_p\cong\mathbb{F}_{p^2}$
$\mathbb{F}_p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{F}_p\cong\mathbb{F}_p$
$\mathbb{F}_p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{F}_p\cong\mathbb{Z}/p^2\mathbb{Z}$
$\mathbb{F}_p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{F}_p\cong 0$
hard.hint: The tensor product over $\mathbb{Z}$ of $\mathbb{F}_p$ with itself is $\mathbb{F}_p$.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ because $\mathbb{Z}/p\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}/p\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(p,p)\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}=\mathbb{F}_p$. Geometrically, $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p=\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_p$: both copies lie over the same closed point $(p)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$.

hard.q: The fiber product $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}\times_{\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{R}}\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ corresponds to $\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C}$. As a $\mathbb{C}$-algebra this splits as $\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{C}$ (two points). What is $\dim_{\mathbb{R}}(\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C})$?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{R}[x]/(x^2+1)$, so $\mathbb{C}\otimes_{\mathbb{R}}\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{C}[x]/(x^2+1)\cong\mathbb{C}[x]/(x-i)(x+i)\cong\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{C}$, which has $\mathbb{R}$-dimension $4$. The base-change from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{C}$ splits the single $\mathbb{R}$-point of $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{C}$ into two $\mathbb{C}$-conjugate points.

hard.q: The universal property of the fiber product $X\times_S Y$ says: morphisms $T\to X\times_S Y$ correspond bijectively to pairs $(f\colon T\to X,\ g\colon T\to Y)$ satisfying which condition?
choices: No condition — any pair works
$f$ and $g$ must both be closed immersions
The compositions $T\to X\to S$ and $T\to Y\to S$ agree
$f=g$ as morphisms
hard.hint: Yoneda applied to the fiber product gives the universal property.
hard.explain: The fiber product is a categorical pullback: $T$-points are pairs $(f,g)$ such that $p_X\circ f = p_Y\circ g\colon T\to S$ (both map to the same point over $S$). This is the defining commutativity of the pullback square.

questions.q: For a morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ and a point $y\in Y$ with residue field $\kappa(y)$, the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_y$ is defined as:
choices: $f^{-1}(y)$ with the subspace topology only
$X\times_Y \operatorname{Spec}\kappa(y)$
$X\sqcup \operatorname{Spec}\kappa(y)$
$\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{O}_{Y,y}/\mathfrak{m}_y)$
questions.explain: The scheme-theoretic fiber is the base change $X\times_Y \operatorname{Spec}\kappa(y)$. Its underlying topological space is $f^{-1}(y)$, but the scheme structure records multiplicities and nilpotents.

questions.q: Consider $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$. How many (geometric, i.e. over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}$) points does the fiber above the closed point $(5)\in\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ have, counted without multiplicity?
questions.explain: The fiber over $(5)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_5[x]/(x^2)$, a single point with a nilpotent — ramified. Topologically the fiber has $1$ point; scheme-theoretically its length is $2$.

questions.q: For the same family $f\colon \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to \operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$, how many geometric points does the fiber over $(11)$ have? (Hint: is $5$ a square mod $11$? Check $4^2=16\equiv 5$.)
questions.explain: Since $5\equiv 4^2\pmod{11}$, the polynomial $x^2-5$ factors as $(x-4)(x+4)$ over $\mathbb{F}_{11}$, so the fiber is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_{11}[x]/(x-4)(x+4)$, which has $2$ reduced points. The prime $11$ splits in $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt 5]$.

hard.q: For $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2-5)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$, the fiber over the generic point $(0)$ is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$. What is the correct sequence of fiber types as the prime $p$ varies?
choices: All fibers are two distinct geometric points

The fiber over $p=5$ is a single point of multiplicity $2$ (ramified); over a prime $p$ with $5$ a non-square mod $p$ it is a single closed point (inert); over a prime $p$ with $5$ a square mod $p$ it is two distinct points (split)
The fiber is always a single geometric point for all primes
The fiber has three points for inert primes and two points for split primes
hard.hint: At the generic point, tensor with $\mathbb{Q}$; it splits if $5$ is a square.
hard.explain: The three behaviors of a prime in a quadratic extension $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{5}]$: ramified ($p=5$, fiber is a fat point of length $2$), inert (the Legendre symbol $(5/p)=-1$, fiber is a single point with residue field $\mathbb{F}_{p^2}$), or split ($(5/p)=1$, fiber is two reduced $\mathbb{F}_p$-points).

hard.q: The fiber of $f\colon\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Z}$ over the inert prime $(3)$ (since $-1$ is not a square mod $3$) is $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{F}_9$. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_3}\mathbb{F}_9$?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{F}_9=\mathbb{F}_{3^2}$ is a degree-$2$ extension of $\mathbb{F}_3$, so $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_3}\mathbb{F}_9=2$. Inert primes give irreducible fibers whose residue field is a degree-$2$ extension of $\mathbb{F}_p$.

hard.q: A morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ has geometrically connected fibers if $X_y\times_{\kappa(y)}\overline{\kappa(y)}$ is connected for every $y\in Y$. Which is a counterexample where fibers are connected but NOT geometrically connected?
choices: $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{R}}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{R}$ — the fiber over the unique point is $\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{R}}$, which is geometrically connected
$\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$ — the fiber is one point over $\mathbb{Q}$, but after base change to $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ it becomes two points
$\mathbb{P}^1_{\mathbb{Q}}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$ — the fiber is $\mathbb{P}^1_{\mathbb{Q}}$, which is geometrically connected
$\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$ — the fiber is a single point, trivially geometrically connected
hard.hint: Base change to the algebraic closure to test geometric connectedness.
hard.explain: $\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)\to\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb{Q}$ has a single-point fiber (connected) but $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt 2)\otimes_{\mathbb{Q}}\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\cong\overline{\mathbb{Q}}\times\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$, which has two connected components. Thus the fiber is connected but not geometrically connected.

questions.q: A morphism $f\colon X\to S$ is separated iff the diagonal $\Delta_f\colon X\to X\times_S X$ is:
choices: an open immersion
a closed immersion
surjective
an isomorphism
questions.explain: Separatedness = closed diagonal. This is the scheme-theoretic analog of Hausdorff, where in topology the diagonal is closed iff limits are unique.

questions.q: Which of the following schemes over $\operatorname{Spec} k$ fails to be separated?
choices: $\mathbb{A}^1_k$
$\mathbb{P}^1_k$
the affine line with doubled origin (two copies of $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ glued along $\mathbb{A}^1_k\setminus\{0\}$)
$\operatorname{Spec} k[x,y]/(xy)$
questions.explain: The line with doubled origin is the canonical non-separated scheme: both origins are limits of the same punctured line, so the diagonal of the structure map is not closed. All other options are separated.

questions.q: Which of these morphisms is proper?
choices: $\mathbb{A}^n_k\to \operatorname{Spec} k$
$\mathbb{P}^n_k\to \operatorname{Spec} k$
$\mathbb{G}_m = \operatorname{Spec} k[x,x^{-1}]\to \operatorname{Spec} k$
a non-closed open immersion
questions.explain: Projective space is the prototype proper morphism over a field. Affine space fails the valuative criterion (a $K$-point like $1/t\in\mathbb{A}^1_K$ does not extend to the DVR), as does $\mathbb{G}_m$.

hard.q: The valuative criterion for properness states: $f\colon X\to S$ is proper (separated + finite type + universally closed) iff for every DVR $R$ with fraction field $K$, every commutative square $\operatorname{Spec} K\to X$, $\operatorname{Spec} R\to S$ has a unique filling $\operatorname{Spec} R\to X$. Which example demonstrates that $\mathbb{A}^1_k\to\operatorname{Spec} k$ fails this criterion?
choices: The $K$-point $\operatorname{Spec} K\to\mathbb{A}^1_k$ given by $t\mapsto 0$ does not extend over $R=k[[t]]$
The $K$-point $\operatorname{Spec} K\to\mathbb{A}^1_k$ given by $x=1/t$ does not extend over $R=k[[t]]$ since $1/t\notin k[[t]]$
$\mathbb{A}^1_k$ is not of finite type over $k$
Every DVR filling exists but is not unique
hard.hint: Existence first, then uniqueness of the extension.
hard.explain: The $K$-rational point $x=1/t\in K=k((t))$ gives a map $\operatorname{Spec} K\to\mathbb{A}^1_k$. To extend to $R=k[[t]]$ we would need $1/t\in k[[t]]$, which is false since $1/t$ has a pole. So no filling exists, and $\mathbb{A}^1_k\to\operatorname{Spec} k$ is not proper.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of logical implications in the proof that a composition of proper morphisms is proper?
choices: Universally closed is stable under composition $\Rightarrow$ separated is stable under composition $\Rightarrow$ finite type is stable under composition $\Rightarrow$ proper is stable

Finite type is stable under composition (straightforward), separated is stable (diagonal factors through $X\times_S X\times_S Y$), universally closed is stable (closed maps compose) $\Rightarrow$ all three conditions compose $\Rightarrow$ proper composes
Proper morphisms compose only when the base is Noetherian
Properness is not stable under composition in general
hard.hint: Composition of closed immersions with proper base changes stays proper.
hard.explain: Each defining condition of properness (finite type, separated, universally closed) is stable under composition. Finite type: $\mathcal{O}$-algebras of finite type compose. Separated: the diagonal of the composition factors through a closed immersion. Universally closed: if $f$ and $g$ are universally closed then so is $g\circ f$ (closed sets map to closed sets through each step).

hard.q: The diagonal $\Delta\colon\mathbb{P}^1_k\to\mathbb{P}^1_k\times_k\mathbb{P}^1_k$ is a closed immersion. The image of $\Delta$ is the diagonal divisor in the surface $\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1$. What is the self-intersection number $\Delta\cdot\Delta$ on $\mathbb{P}^1\times\mathbb{P}^1$? (Hint: use $\Delta^2=\deg(T_{\mathbb{P}^1})=\chi(\mathbb{P}^1)$.)
hard.hint: Diagonal image is the anti-diagonal-complement inside $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$.
hard.explain: The self-intersection of the diagonal equals the degree of the tangent bundle $T_{\mathbb{P}^1}$, which equals $\chi(\mathbb{P}^1)=2$ (by Euler characteristic, $\chi(\mathbb{P}^1)=2$ since $\mathbb{P}^1\cong S^2$). Alternatively, $T_{\mathbb{P}^1}\cong\mathcal{O}(2)$ has degree $2$.
